title: The Inner Safety Scan
subtitle: A five-minute body scan that looks for the parts of the body that are already calm
excerpt: A five-minute body-scan meditation that reverses the standard scan: instead of looking for tension, it looks for the parts of the body that are already at ease. Used when the whole body feels keyed up.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The body-scan tradition draws on secular mindfulness adaptations of Buddhist contemplative practice (the form most readers will know is the Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction body scan developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn in the late 1970s). The reversed-attention shape used here is closer to the resourcing practices taught in Peter Levine's somatic experiencing work.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the whole body feels keyed up and a standard body scan would only confirm what you already know. Looking for the part that is already settled gives the nervous system somewhere to land.

content.text: A five-minute meditation that reverses the standard body scan. Instead of looking for tension, the attention searches the body for one part that is already at ease and rests there.
Sit or lie down. The eyes can close or stay soft. Take one slow breath.
Start at the soles of the feet. Notice what is there. The temperature of the skin against the floor or against the inside of a shoe. The weight of the body pressing down. Stay for two breaths.
Move up to the calves. Then the knees. Then the thighs. At each stop, ask one question: is this part of the body easy right now?
Keep going. The lower back. The belly. The chest. The shoulders. The arms. The hands. The neck. The jaw. The eyes. The forehead. The scalp.

Somewhere on the way through, the answer to the question will be yes. The little toe, the inside of the wrist, the back of the knee. The body always has a part that is currently at ease, even when most of it is keyed up. Find it.
When you find the easy part, stop. Stay with it for the rest of the five minutes. The attention sits where the ease is. The rest of the body is allowed to be whatever it is.
Open the eyes if they were closed. Take one more breath into the part of the body you found. Carry on with the day.

Original to homemade.education. The body scan as a practice has many lineages. The version most readers will know is the body scan taught in Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (Jon Kabat-Zinn, late 1970s). The shape used here is the reverse: look for the part of the body that is already at ease, not for the parts that are tense. That move is closer to the resourcing work in Peter Levine's somatic teaching.
